content.text: A robot that cannot identify and locate obstacles in its path cannot navigate safely. Object detection bridges the gap between raw sensor data and the actionable geometric information that a path planner requires: which cells in the costmap are occupied, how fast they are moving, and whether a detected region is a pedestrian, a pallet, or a static wall. Getting detection right is therefore a prerequisite for every mobile robot application, from warehouse AMRs to surgical assistants.

Object detection is the task of simultaneously locating and classifying objects within sensor data. In robotics it most commonly operates on camera images (producing 2D bounding boxes), LiDAR point clouds (producing 3D oriented bounding boxes), or a fusion of both. The output — a set of detected objects each with a class label, confidence score, and spatial extent — feeds directly into the robot's navigation stack, where it populates obstacle costmaps and triggers reactive behaviours.

Navigation is the broader problem of moving the robot from its current pose to a goal pose while avoiding obstacles. Modern navigation stacks (Nav2 in ROS 2) decouple the problem into global planning (find a path through the known static map), local planning (follow the path while reacting to dynamic obstacles within sensor range), and recovery behaviours (escape from costmap deadlocks). Object detection feeds the local planner's costmap with dynamic obstacle footprints.

Static maps alone are insufficient for real-world deployment. People walk through warehouses, forklifts appear unexpectedly, and temporary obstructions block planned paths. A robot that relies only on a pre-built static map will collide with dynamic obstacles or freeze indefinitely when the map diverges from reality. Real-time object detection provides the dynamic obstacle layer that makes navigation robust to the changing world.

Beyond avoidance, class-aware detection enables semantic navigation: a hospital robot that can identify doors, elevators, and humans can plan semantically appropriate routes — avoiding crowded corridors, waiting for doors to open, and slowing near patients — rather than treating all occupied space as uniformly forbidden.
Real-time obstacle identification: YOLO-family detectors run at 30+ FPS on a single GPU, well within the navigation control loop.
Class-aware costmaps: knowing an obstacle is a person vs. a static crate allows different inflation radii and velocity constraints.
3D bounding boxes from LiDAR provide precise footprint and heading for moving objects, enabling predictive collision avoidance.
Multi-object tracking (SORT, DeepSORT) assigns persistent IDs and velocity estimates, enabling trajectory prediction.
Pre-trained backbone models (COCO, Open Images) transfer well to robotic domains with minimal fine-tuning.
Transformer-based detectors (DETR) eliminate hand-tuned anchors and NMS, simplifying the detection pipeline.
Latency vs. accuracy trade-off: larger models (Faster R-CNN, DETR) are more accurate but slower; YOLO trades some accuracy for speed.
Domain gap: detectors trained on COCO miss robotic-specific objects (pallets, charging docks, conveyor belts) unless fine-tuned.
2D detection does not provide depth: projecting 2D boxes into the robot's 3D world requires LiDAR or a depth camera for range.
False positives in costmaps cause unnecessary detours or freezes; false negatives miss real obstacles and can cause collisions.
Tracking identity switches (ID swaps) after occlusion confuse the trajectory predictor, leading to wrong velocity estimates.
GPU dependency: real-time inference on embedded hardware (Jetson, Raspberry Pi) requires model quantisation (INT8/FP16) and TensorRT optimisation.
Modern 2D detectors follow either a two-stage or one-stage design.

is the canonical two-stage detector: a Region Proposal Network (RPN) first proposes candidate regions (anchors), then a second network classifies and refines each proposal. This produces high accuracy but adds latency (50–200 ms per frame on a GPU).

is a single-stage detector that divides the image into a grid, predicts bounding boxes and class probabilities for each cell in a single forward pass, and applies Non-Maximum Suppression to resolve overlapping predictions. YOLOv8 achieves state-of-the-art speed-accuracy trade-offs — 3–30 ms on a GPU depending on model size.

DETR (Detection Transformer) replaces the anchor and NMS machinery entirely with a transformer encoder-decoder. The encoder attends to global image features; a fixed set of learned object queries in the decoder produce predictions directly via set prediction loss (Hungarian matching). DETR has excellent accuracy and a clean architecture, but its original version is slow to train and converge; Deformable DETR and RT-DETR address these issues for real-time use.

encodes point clouds as vertical pillars (pseudo-images) and applies a 2D CNN, achieving 60 Hz on a GPU. VoxelNet and its successors voxelise the point cloud and apply 3D sparse convolutions. These 3D detectors output oriented 3D bounding boxes — essential for accurate footprint estimation and heading prediction needed by the collision-avoidance layer.
Multi-object tracking associates detections across frames.
uses a Kalman filter per track and the Hungarian algorithm to associate new detections with existing tracks by IoU overlap.

adds a CNN appearance feature (Re-ID embedding) to reduce identity switches after occlusion. ByteTrack (2022) improves association by including low-confidence detections in the first pass, significantly reducing false negatives in crowded scenes.

attrs.alt: Object detection to navigation pipeline showing camera and LiDAR feeding 2D and 3D detectors, then tracker, then costmap, then Nav2 planner

content.text: Camera and LiDAR data are processed in parallel by 2D and 3D detectors. Detections are fused and passed to the multi-object tracker, which maintains track states across frames. Track footprints populate the navigation costmap's dynamic obstacle layer, and the Nav2 local planner (DWB) replans the velocity command on every control tick.
Any mobile robot operating in shared human spaces where dynamic obstacles must be classified and avoided.
Autonomous vehicles requiring 3D bounding box estimates for surrounding vehicles and pedestrians.
Manipulation robots that must detect and localise target objects for pick-and-place.
Inspection drones needing to classify infrastructure elements (pipes, bolts, cracks) in addition to navigating.
Agricultural robots identifying crop rows, weeds, or fruit for targeted actions.
The standard ROS 2 integration path uses the
package for obstacle layer updates. The
and marks cells as occupied. For class-aware dynamic obstacles, a custom costmap plugin receives
and inflates each detected box footprint with a configurable radius based on object class.

"""YOLOv8 inference node for ROS 2 — subscribes to camera image, publishes
Detection2DArray for downstream costmap integration."""
import rclpy
from rclpy.node import Node
from sensor_msgs.msg import Image
from vision_msgs.msg import Detection2DArray, Detection2D, ObjectHypothesisWithPose
from cv_bridge import CvBridge
from ultralytics import YOLO
import numpy as np

CLASS_NAMES = ["person", "bicycle", "car", "motorcycle", "bus", "truck"]

class YoloDetectorNode(Node):
    def __init__(self):
        super().__init__("yolo_detector")
        self.declare_parameter("model_path", "yolov8n.pt")
        self.declare_parameter("confidence_threshold", 0.45)
        self.declare_parameter("device", "cuda:0")

        model_path = self.get_parameter("model_path").value
        self.conf_thresh = self.get_parameter("confidence_threshold").value
        device = self.get_parameter("device").value

        self.model = YOLO(model_path)
        self.model.to(device)
        self.bridge = CvBridge()

        self.sub = self.create_subscription(
            Image, "/camera/image_raw", self.image_callback, 10
        )
        self.pub = self.create_publisher(
            Detection2DArray, "/detections/2d", 10
        )
        self.get_logger().info(f"YOLOv8 detector ready on {device}")

    def image_callback(self, msg: Image):
        cv_img = self.bridge.imgmsg_to_cv2(msg, desired_encoding="bgr8")
        results = self.model(cv_img, verbose=False)[0]

        det_array = Detection2DArray()
        det_array.header = msg.header

        for box in results.boxes:
            if float(box.conf) < self.conf_thresh:
                continue
            x1, y1, x2, y2 = box.xyxy[0].cpu().numpy().astype(int)
            cls_id = int(box.cls)
            conf = float(box.conf)

            det = Detection2D()
            det.header = msg.header
            det.bbox.center.position.x = float((x1 + x2) / 2)
            det.bbox.center.position.y = float((y1 + y2) / 2)
            det.bbox.size_x = float(x2 - x1)
            det.bbox.size_y = float(y2 - y1)

            hyp = ObjectHypothesisWithPose()
            hyp.hypothesis.class_id = str(cls_id)
            hyp.hypothesis.score = conf
            det.results.append(hyp)
            det_array.detections.append(det)

        self.pub.publish(det_array)

def main():
    rclpy.init()
    node = YoloDetectorNode()
    rclpy.spin(node)
    rclpy.shutdown()

if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()

"""SORT multi-object tracker adapted for ROS 2.
Subscribes to Detection2DArray, maintains Kalman tracks, publishes
persistent track IDs with velocity for downstream nav costmap."""
import numpy as np
from scipy.optimize import linear_sum_assignment
from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from typing import List

@dataclass
class KalmanTrack:
    """State: [cx, cy, w, h, vx, vy, vw, vh]"""
    state: np.ndarray   # 8-vector
    P: np.ndarray       # 8x8 covariance
    track_id: int
    age: int = 0
    hits: int = 1
    time_since_update: int = 0

    # Transition and measurement matrices
    F: np.ndarray = field(default_factory=lambda: np.eye(8) + np.eye(8, k=4))
    H: np.ndarray = field(default_factory=lambda: np.eye(4, 8))

    def predict(self):
        self.state = self.F @ self.state
        self.P = self.F @ self.P @ self.F.T + np.eye(8) * 1e-2
        self.age += 1
        self.time_since_update += 1

    def update(self, z: np.ndarray):
        """z = [cx, cy, w, h]"""
        y = z - self.H @ self.state
        S = self.H @ self.P @ self.H.T + np.eye(4) * 1.0
        K = self.P @ self.H.T @ np.linalg.inv(S)
        self.state = self.state + K @ y
        self.P = (np.eye(8) - K @ self.H) @ self.P
        self.hits += 1
        self.time_since_update = 0

class SORTTracker:
    def __init__(self, max_age: int = 3, min_hits: int = 2, iou_threshold: float = 0.3):
        self.tracks: List[KalmanTrack] = []
        self.next_id = 1
        self.max_age = max_age
        self.min_hits = min_hits
        self.iou_threshold = iou_threshold

    def _iou(self, a: np.ndarray, b: np.ndarray) -> float:
        """Boxes as [cx, cy, w, h]."""
        ax1, ay1 = a[0]-a[2]/2, a[1]-a[3]/2
        ax2, ay2 = a[0]+a[2]/2, a[1]+a[3]/2
        bx1, by1 = b[0]-b[2]/2, b[1]-b[3]/2
        bx2, by2 = b[0]+b[2]/2, b[1]+b[3]/2
        inter_w = max(0, min(ax2, bx2) - max(ax1, bx1))
        inter_h = max(0, min(ay2, by2) - max(ay1, by1))
        inter = inter_w * inter_h
        union = a[2]*a[3] + b[2]*b[3] - inter + 1e-6
        return inter / union

    def update(self, detections: np.ndarray) -> np.ndarray:
        """detections: (N, 4) as [cx, cy, w, h]. Returns (M, 5) as [cx,cy,w,h,id]."""
        for t in self.tracks:
            t.predict()

        if len(self.tracks) == 0 or len(detections) == 0:
            assigned_det = set()
        else:
            iou_mat = np.array([
                [self._iou(detections[d], self.tracks[t].state[:4])
                 for t in range(len(self.tracks))]
                for d in range(len(detections))
            ])
            row_ind, col_ind = linear_sum_assignment(-iou_mat)
            assigned_det = set()
            for r, c in zip(row_ind, col_ind):
                if iou_mat[r, c] >= self.iou_threshold:
                    self.tracks[c].update(detections[r])
                    assigned_det.add(r)

        for d in range(len(detections)):
            if d not in assigned_det:
                s = np.zeros(8)
                s[:4] = detections[d]
                self.tracks.append(KalmanTrack(
                    state=s, P=np.eye(8)*10.0, track_id=self.next_id
                ))
                self.next_id += 1

        self.tracks = [t for t in self.tracks if t.time_since_update <= self.max_age]
        return np.array([
            [*t.state[:4], t.track_id]
            for t in self.tracks if t.hits >= self.min_hits
        ]) if self.tracks else np.empty((0, 5))

"""Feed tracked detections into Nav2 costmap as dynamic obstacles.
Subscribes to SORT track output and publishes ObstacleArrayMsg
(nav2_dynamic_msgs) for the DynamicObstacleLayer."""
import rclpy
from rclpy.node import Node
from vision_msgs.msg import Detection3DArray
from geometry_msgs.msg import Point32
from nav2_dynamic_msgs.msg import ObstacleArray, Obstacle

CLASS_INFLATION = {"person": 0.6, "car": 1.5, "default": 0.5}  # metres

class CostmapFeeder(Node):
    def __init__(self):
        super().__init__("costmap_feeder")
        self.sub = self.create_subscription(
            Detection3DArray, "/detections/3d_tracked", self.cb, 10
        )
        self.pub = self.create_publisher(ObstacleArray, "/dynamic_obstacles", 10)

    def cb(self, msg: Detection3DArray):
        out = ObstacleArray()
        out.header = msg.header
        for det in msg.detections:
            cls = det.results[0].hypothesis.class_id if det.results else "default"
            radius = CLASS_INFLATION.get(cls, CLASS_INFLATION["default"])
            obs = Obstacle()
            obs.uuid = det.id
            obs.position = det.bbox.center.position
            obs.linear_velocity = det.results[0].pose.pose.position  # velocity packed here
            obs.radius = radius
            out.obstacles.append(obs)
        self.pub.publish(out)

def main():
    rclpy.init()
    rclpy.spin(CostmapFeeder())
    rclpy.shutdown()

if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()

Amazon Robotics' Proteus autonomous mobile robot uses camera-based person detection as a critical safety layer. The robot must detect and stop for humans within a defined protective zone while operating in fulfilment centres. The detection pipeline runs at 30 Hz with a hard latency budget of under 100 ms end-to-end — from camera exposure to emergency-stop signal. When a person is detected within 2 m, the robot slows to a crawl; if the person is within 0.5 m, it stops entirely. The costmap integration writes the person footprint with maximum cost (254) in the local costmap, which both prevents the path planner from routing through the person and triggers the DWB local planner to decelerate.
Running detection at camera frame rate (30 Hz) when the navigation stack only needs 10 Hz: unnecessary GPU load; throttle the detection rate.
Publishing detections in camera frame and forgetting to transform to the robot's base_link or map frame before inserting into the costmap.
Using IoU-only tracking (SORT) in scenes with many occlusions — leads to frequent ID switches and incorrect velocity estimates.
Not inflating obstacle footprints: marking only the exact bounding box leaves no safety margin; the robot will path-plan through the person's boundary.
Trusting detections from a fixed confidence threshold regardless of context — a 0.45 threshold that is fine for COCO may be too low for a class with many false positives in your environment.
Fine-tune a pre-trained YOLO or Faster R-CNN on domain-specific data (your robot's camera, lighting, and environment) before deployment.
Use TensorRT FP16 or INT8 optimisation for deployment on Jetson to reduce inference latency 2–4x.
Combine 2D camera detection with LiDAR range: project LiDAR points into the 2D bounding boxes to obtain accurate 3D distance.
Set class-specific inflation radii in the costmap — pedestrians need more margin than static poles.
Log all detections with timestamps for post-mission analysis and dataset collection for further fine-tuning.
Validate end-to-end latency on the actual deployment hardware before mission — model benchmarks measured on a desktop GPU do not transfer to an embedded board.

Latency is the primary performance concern in robotic detection. At 10 Hz control, the entire detection-to-costmap pipeline must complete within 100 ms. YOLOv8n on a Jetson Orin with TensorRT INT8 achieves approximately 12 ms, leaving comfortable headroom. YOLOv8x (maximum accuracy) requires ~70 ms, consuming most of the budget and leaving little room for tracking and costmap updates. PointPillars for 3D LiDAR detection runs at ~15 ms on the same hardware.

Multi-object tracking scales linearly in the number of tracks for SORT (Hungarian algorithm is O(n³) but n is typically small — under 20 objects). DeepSORT adds a CNN forward pass per detected object for the Re-ID embedding, which can add 5–10 ms per frame with many detections. ByteTrack avoids the Re-ID overhead while still outperforming SORT on occlusion benchmarks.
Exports to TensorRT, ONNX, CoreML; simple Python API
Facebook AI Research; rich evaluation suite; instance segmentation
nuScenes / KITTI / Waymo benchmark baselines included
State-of-the-art MOT20; no Re-ID overhead; Python + C++ implementations
FP16/INT8 quantisation; 2–4x latency reduction on NVIDIA hardware
ObstacleArray feeds Nav2 DynamicObstacleLayer for costmap updates
LiDAR 3D boxes; AV and warehouse robots

Detection-based navigation has safety-critical failure modes. A missed detection (false negative) means an obstacle is not marked in the costmap and the robot may collide. Mitigation includes conservative costmap decay rates (occupied cells persist for a configurable time after the last detection), complementary LiDAR-based obstacle layers that do not depend on classification, and hardware watchdog timers that trigger an emergency stop if the detection node heartbeat is lost.

Adversarial robustness is an emerging concern: physical adversarial patches printed on clothing can fool YOLO-family detectors into misclassifying or missing a person. For safety-critical deployments, detection should be backed by a secondary non-deep sensor (laser curtain, ultrasonic bumpers) that operates independently of the neural detector.
YOLO-family detectors provide the best speed-accuracy trade-off for real-time robotics; choose model size based on the available compute budget.
2D detection must be depth-resolved (via LiDAR projection or stereo) before it can populate a 3D costmap correctly.
SORT provides lightweight tracking; DeepSORT reduces ID switches in occluded scenes; ByteTrack offers the best balance for crowded environments.
Class-aware inflation radii in the costmap let the navigation planner apply appropriate safety margins per object type.
TensorRT INT8 quantisation is the first step when inference latency exceeds the control loop budget on embedded hardware.
Never rely solely on a neural detector for safety — combine with non-learning geometric sensors (LiDAR scan, bumpers) for a defence-in-depth approach.
Fine-tune on domain-specific data; COCO pre-training rarely covers the exact objects and lighting conditions a deployed robot encounters.
